event: Genoese and Portuguese navigators in Portuguese service first sighted the uninhabited Cape Verde islands in the Atlantic.
Navigators including Diogo Gomes and Antonio de Noli reached several of the islands, confirming the archipelago's discovery for Portugal.
Portuguese settlers founded Ribeira Grande on Santiago, the first permanent European town in the tropics.
The Portuguese crown granted Cape Verde settlers a trading monopoly along the nearby West African coast, fueling a slave-based economy.
Cape Verde was formally brought under direct Portuguese crown administration as a royal colony.
Ribeira Grande was elevated to a city and made the seat of a Catholic diocese, becoming a hub of the Atlantic slave trade.
English privateer Francis Drake attacked and sacked Ribeira Grande during his Caribbean raiding expedition.
French forces under Jacques Cassard pillaged Ribeira Grande, hastening the decline of the old capital.
The islands suffered the first of many devastating droughts and famines that would recur over the following centuries.
Praia on Santiago became the de facto administrative center, supplanting the ruined Ribeira Grande.
Charles Darwin visited Cape Verde aboard HMS Beagle, his first landfall on the famous voyage.
Portugal abolished slavery in Cape Verde, ending the institution central to the colony's founding economy.
Portuguese Guinea was administratively separated from Cape Verde, dividing the previously linked territories.
Praia was officially confirmed as the capital city of the colony of Cape Verde.
Catastrophic drought and famine killed a large share of the population amid colonial neglect.
Portugal reclassified Cape Verde from a colony to an overseas province to deflect rising anti-colonial pressure.
Amilcar Cabral and others founded the PAIGC to seek independence for Cape Verde and Guinea-Bissau.
The PAIGC launched an armed liberation war, fought mainly on the mainland of Portuguese Guinea.
Liberation leader Amilcar Cabral was assassinated in Conakry shortly before the movement's goals were realized.
Portugal's Carnation Revolution opened the way for decolonization and negotiations on Cape Verde's future.
Cape Verde gained independence on 5 July with Aristides Pereira as president and Pedro Pires as prime minister.
Cape Verde was admitted to the United Nations as a sovereign member state.
A coup in Guinea-Bissau ended plans to unite the two countries, prompting the PAIGC's Cape Verde branch to reorganize as the PAICV.
The PAICV was formally established as Cape Verde's sole legal party in a one-party state.
The National Assembly abolished one-party rule, paving the way for multiparty democracy.
The opposition MpD won the first free elections, and Antonio Mascarenhas Monteiro became president in a peaceful transfer of power.
A new democratic constitution was adopted, and the country changed its flag and national emblem.
The PAICV returned to power and Pedro Pires was elected president in tightly contested elections.
Cape Verde graduated from the UN's list of least developed countries, a rare development milestone.
Cape Verde joined the World Trade Organization, deepening its integration into the global economy.
Ribeira Grande (Cidade Velha) was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site as the first colonial European outpost in the tropics.
Jorge Carlos Fonseca of the MpD was elected president, succeeding Pedro Pires.
The MpD won parliamentary elections and Ulisses Correia e Silva became prime minister, while Fonseca was reelected president.
Jose Maria Neves of the PAICV was elected president, and the MpD retained its parliamentary majority.
